prompt:
User: I’ve been a Christian all my life. I’m currently 16 now and have my heart set on being a veterinarian. My walk with God has taken a huge hit recently because I found out that my animals are not coming with me in the rapture. They are left here to suffer the horrible fate of all the unbelievers. It’s a lonely, painful, sad, horrible death and I just don’t see why God would inflict that on a little puppy or even a lion. God is supposed to be merciful and loving! How is that any of this? I can’t get over the fact that my poor little maltipoo is going to be left here to die all alone and afraid when I’m taken up. Why would God do that?
Answer:
completion: There is no need to be concerned about the rapture because the Bible does not teach that. 1 Thessalonians 4:17 (“After that, we who are still alive and are left will be caught up together with them in the clouds to meet the Lord in the air.”) is describing what will take place on the Last Day. The context of that verse is clearly describing events associated with Jesus’ visible return on the Last Day. The Bible does not teach a whisking away of Christians prior to the Last Day.  While God created animals, people are the crown of his creation (Genesis 1 and 2). It is people who have bodies and souls. It is possible that animals will be part of the new heaven and new earth (Revelation 21:1), but Scripture does not explicitly state that.  I wish you well as you pursue a vocation in veterinary medicine. You can serve God and others by using your God-given abilities in that career.

prompt:

Answer:
completion: Test response - Faith is the assurance of things hoped for.

prompt:
User: How can i use the bible to disapprove evolution?
Answer:
completion: To address the question of how to use the Bible to disapprove of evolution, we can look to Scripture and Lutheran theology for guidance.  First and foremost, the biblical account of creation in Genesis is foundational. Genesis 1:26-27 states that God created humankind in His own image, male and female. This teaching emphasizes that humans are a distinct creation of God, made with purpose and dignity. The WELS (Wisconsin Evangelical Lutheran Synod) teaching affirms that God created the world in six days and that everything He made was "very good" (Genesis 1:31). This direct act of creation contrasts with the evolutionary theory, which posits that life developed over millions of years through random processes.  In "This We Believe," it is stated that God is the creator of all things and that His creation is intentional and purposeful (This We Believe, Creation, Man, and Sin). This belief underscores the idea that life did not arise by chance but through the deliberate action of God. The doctrine of creation as taught in WELS emphasizes that God’s Word is authoritative and that we can trust it as the true account of how the world and humanity came to be.  Additionally, Romans 1:20 teaches that God's invisible qualities, His eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly seen through what has been made. This suggests that the created order reflects God's intentional design, which stands in contrast to the randomness implied by evolutionary theory.  In summary, to disapprove of evolution using the Bible, one can highlight the clear biblical teaching on creation as a direct act of God, the special creation of humanity in His image, and the authority of Scripture in revealing God’s creative work. These points align with WELS teachings and provide a solid foundation for understanding creation in a way that counters evolutionary explanations.  If you have questions about this teaching or how it applies to your life, please speak with your pastor or a trusted church leader for personal guidance.

prompt:
User: Will we ever know if Angels were around us right now?
Answer:
completion: The question of whether we will ever know if angels are around us right now touches on the nature of angels and God's revelation to us. According to Scripture, angels are spiritual beings created by God to serve Him and assist His people (Hebrews 1:14). They are often described as messengers and protectors, but their presence is not always visible to us.  While Scripture teaches that angels are present in the world and actively involved in God's plan, it does not provide us with the ability to perceive them at all times. For example, in the book of Hebrews, we are reminded that angels are ministering spirits sent to serve those who will inherit salvation (Hebrews 1:14). However, their work is often unseen and operates in the spiritual realm.  As for knowing their presence, the Bible does not promise that we will have a clear awareness of angels around us. Instead, it emphasizes trusting in God's protection and guidance, which may include the work of angels, even if we cannot see them. According to WELS teaching, we can be assured that God is always with us, and His angels may be part of that divine care, but we are not given specific knowledge of their presence at any given moment.  In summary, while angels are indeed present and active according to Scripture, we will not necessarily know if they are around us at any specific time. Our focus should remain on trusting in God's promises and His care for us, rather than seeking to identify the unseen spiritual beings that may be at work.  If you have questions about this teaching or how it applies to your life, please speak with your pastor or a trusted church leader for personal guidance.
